parents.mother_research_notes: Margaret Hart — daughter of Capt. Thomas Hart (X00031), son of Stephen Hart (X00034)
parents.father_research_notes: Asahel Strong Sr. (b. Nov 14, 1668 Northampton MA) — married Margaret Hart, daughter of Capt. Thomas Hart (X00031)

cross_connections.display_label: Both Strong men who married Hooker descendants, 84 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage echoed down two more generations
narrative_blocks.body: When Captain Asahel Strong Jr. married Sarah Hooker in 1729 at Farmington, Connecticut — a great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — he began a pattern that would echo through generations: their great-great-grandson Asahel Hooker Strong was born in 1776 with 'Hooker' preserved as his middle name, and 84 years after the first marriage, Judge Elisha Beebe Strong married another Thomas Hooker descendant, Dolly Goodwin Hooker, at Canandaigua, New York in 1813. By 1866, that second union had produced Beebe Hooker Strong of Milwaukee, the fourth person in the Strong family to carry the Hooker name — connecting the original Farmington parsonage to the Wisconsin frontier across 137 years.

narrative_blocks.header: His Hooker marriage echoed down two more generations
narrative_blocks.body: When Captain Asahel Strong Jr. married Sarah Hooker in 1729 at Farmington, Connecticut — a great-great-granddaughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker — he began a pattern that would echo through generations: their great-great-grandson Asahel Hooker Strong was born in 1776 with 'Hooker' preserved as his middle name, and 84 years after the first marriage, Judge Elisha Beebe Strong married another Thomas Hooker descendant, Dolly Goodwin Hooker, at Canandaigua, New York in 1813. By 1866, that second union had produced Beebe Hooker Strong of Milwaukee, the fourth person in the Strong family to carry the Hooker name — connecting the original Farmington parsonage to the Wisconsin frontier across 137 years.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker women who married Strongs in 1729 and 1813

narrative_blocks.header: Eight children with Capt. Asahel Strong Jr.
narrative_blocks.body: She married Capt. Asahel Strong Jr. on January 8, 1729 and bore him eight children — including Rev. Cyprian Strong of Chatham. Asahel was a grandson of Stephen Hart through his mother Margaret Hart, closing another loop in the Hart-Hooker network.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost three children in September 1736
narrative_blocks.body: In a single ten-day span — September 6 to 15, 1736 — three of her young children died: Lois and Ruth on the same day, then Elnathan nine days later. The cluster strongly suggests an epidemic moving through Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Second husband Solomon Whitman lived to 93
narrative_blocks.body: After Capt. Asahel Strong's death she married Solomon Whitman, who was Town Clerk at 92 and made a pair of shoes on his 90th birthday. He died at 93 from over-fatigue settling a fence dispute between two brothers.

narrative_blocks.header: Lois died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lois died in 1776, aged about ten. Lois’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband organized first Episcopal parish in Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Sally's husband Rev. Roger Searle organized the first Episcopal parish in Ohio — St. Peter's at Ashtabula on February 19, 1817 — and preached the first religious service in Medina County Ohio on March 11, 1817. He went on to organize Trinity Cleveland, St. Mark's Columbia, St. James' Boardman, and St. Luke's Ravenna. Sally lived alongside this Ohio Episcopal circuit-rider mission through its founding decade.

narrative_blocks.header: Widowed at Ohio frontier age fifty-four
narrative_blocks.body: Sally was widowed at age fifty-four when Rev. Searle died September 6, 1826 in Ashtabula Ohio. She lived another twenty-three years and ultimately died in 1849 at Ogdensburg, Saint Lawrence County New York — having migrated from her Ohio widowhood home northeast to the Saint Lawrence River frontier. The Connecticut-to-Ohio-to-Ogdensburg migration arc spanned three frontier regions across her seventy-six years.

narrative_blocks.header: Two children: son Lucian and daughter Nancy
narrative_blocks.body: Sally and Rev. Searle had two documented children. Their son Lucian Theodoscius Searle (1808-1895) lived to age eighty-seven; their daughter Nancy Sarah Marie Searle Johnston (1817-1906) lived to age eighty-eight or eighty-nine. Both children outlived the parents by half a century and represent the surviving Searle line through Sally Pomeroy's marriage.

narrative_blocks.header: Middletown CT 1772 to Ogdensburg NY 1849
narrative_blocks.body: Sally Pomeroy was born April 21, 1772 in Middletown Connecticut, daughter of Adino Pomeroy and Lois Strong Pomeroy. She married Rev. Roger Searle on August 7, 1800 and died January 17, 1849 at Ogdensburg, Saint Lawrence County New York at age seventy-six. She is buried at Ogdensburg Cemetery.

sources.short: Orderly Book of Capt. Ichabod Norton
sources.notes: Original MS owned by Charles Mulford Robinson, Rochester NY, 1909.

military_service.notes: Ruth Strong Norton — wife of Col. Ichabod Norton.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband served under her Hooker cousin in 1777
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Major Ichabod Norton entered the Revolutionary army as a lieutenant in 1776, fighting at Skeensborough, Fort Ann, and Ticonderoga. In 1777 at Peekskill he served under Colonel Noadiah Hooker — Ruth's first cousin once removed in the Hooker line — and was eventually promoted to Major.

narrative_blocks.header: Two Ruths — only one survived past four
narrative_blocks.body: Her parents' first daughter Ruth had died at age four on September 6, 1736; she was born three years later in 1739 and given the same name. Replacement naming was a common Puritan practice when an infant died young, but it left the second Ruth carrying her dead sister's identity for life.

cross_connections.display_label: Served under Colonel Hooker at Peekskill in spring 1777.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife died the day their son
narrative_blocks.body: Belinda Porter died on February 22, 1792 — the same day their son Ichabod Porter Norton was born. Ichabod survived his mother by just 21 years, dying in 1813.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the Governor's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Belinda's death, he married Dorothy Treadwell — daughter of Gov. John Treadwell, the 21st Governor of Connecticut. Through that marriage, and through his first cousin once removed Maj. Roger Hooker's marriage to the Governor's sister Mary, two members of the Hooker family had independently married into the Treadwell household.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins once removed — both married into the Treadwell family.
his father-in-law, a governor of Connecticut

burial.plot_notes: Gravestone: "Rev. Cyprian Strong, D.D. Died Nov. 17th. 1811 In the 68th year of his age, And 44th of his ministry; In morals, exemplary; In doctrine, uncorrupt; In reasoning, profound; In declaring Gods counsel, Perspicuous and solemn; And in death, peaceful."

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

narrative_blocks.header: His gravestone counts 44 years of ministry
narrative_blocks.body: He was ordained at Chatham (now Portland) on August 9, 1767 and remained its Congregational pastor until his death on November 17, 1811 — 44 years in the same pulpit. His gravestone records both: "In the 68th year of his age, And 44th of his ministry; In morals, exemplary; In doctrine, uncorrupt; In reasoning, profound."

narrative_blocks.header: Yale or Harvard? — the record says Yale
narrative_blocks.body: EH, the Strong family book, and Yale archival records all confirm he graduated Yale 1763. The Native Northeast Portal biography states Harvard — this appears to be an error in that source. He served as Farmington Town Clerk 1764-67 immediately after graduation, before ordination.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter walked the Trail of Tears
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Sarah Strong married Judge John Ellsworth; their daughter Harriet Ellsworth married Ainsworth Blunt at Brainerd Mission, Tennessee, where they ministered to the Cherokees. When the forced removal came in 1838, Blunt accompanied the Cherokees on the Trail of Tears before illness forced him back from the Mississippi. He later became the first mayor of Dalton, Georgia.

education.notes: EH, Strong family book (5345-8), and Yale archival collection all confirm Yale 1763. Native Northeast Portal biography states Harvard — this appears to be an error in that source. Sprague Annals of the American Pulpit vol. I pp. 651-3 contains a life sketch.

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

narrative_blocks.header: Florella died young
narrative_blocks.body: Florella died in 1775, aged about six. Florella’s parents gave the name to a child born four years later.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter Harriet walked the Trail of Tears
narrative_blocks.body: Her daughter Harriet Ellsworth traveled to Brainerd Mission in Tennessee as a young woman and met Ainsworth Blunt there. They married in 1822 and spent fifteen years ministering to the Cherokees. When the forced removal came in 1838, Blunt accompanied the tribe on the Trail of Tears until illness forced him back.

cross_connections.display_label: Both bore the Hooker name through the Strong family, 90 years apart

narrative_blocks.header: Named Hooker to preserve his grandmother's birth name
narrative_blocks.body: Asahel Hooker Strong was born on November 8, 1776, in Chatham, Connecticut — the son of Reverend Cyprian Strong D.D. and the grandson of Captain Asahel Strong Jr. and Sarah Hooker Strong of Farmington; his middle name 'Hooker' explicitly preserved the maiden name of a Thomas Hooker descendant, making him the first Strong to carry that name as a living act of genealogical memory. Ninety years later, his distant cousin Beebe Hooker Strong was born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin — the second Strong to be named 'Hooker,' and a testament to how deliberately this frontier family remembered its Connecticut origins.

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

cross_connections.display_label: Both on the Farmington Library founding committee; Hooker a borrower.

parents.father_research_notes: Jonathan Bull of Farmington CT (EH entry 300)

narrative_blocks.header: Two siblings married two siblings — one ceremony
narrative_blocks.body: On November 9, 1768 in Farmington, Cyprian Strong married Sarah Bull, and his sister Elizabeth Strong married Sarah's brother Martin Bull — both weddings on the same day. Two Strong siblings and two Bull siblings, bound together in a single ceremony.

narrative_blocks.header: He engraved Farmington's Monthly Library bookplates
narrative_blocks.body: A goldsmith, silversmith (silver spoons and buttons), and gunpowder manufacturer, Martin Bull also served as church deacon, conductor of church music, Probate Clerk of Farmington for 39 years, and Town Treasurer for 8. As an engraver he designed the Farmington Monthly Library bookplate around 1801 — described as 'one of the more memorable American bookplates of the early Federal period.'

narrative_blocks.header: Among 70 Farmington men pledged at Ticonderoga, 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Before the Revolution formally began, he was among 70 Farmington signers of an agreement to march to Boston to assist besieged countrymen if needed. He later manufactured saltpetre for the Continental Army.

burial.plot_notes: Andover Road Cemetery, Hebron, Tolland County, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Catherine died young
narrative_blocks.body: Catherine died in 1782, aged about eleven.
